era_context: The early 13th century is characterized by the Mongol expansion under Genghis Khan and his successors, the consolidation of European feudalism and early nation-states, the height of the Crusades, and the flourishing of Islamic and Christian scholasticism. The Jin Dynasty in northern China is under severe pressure from Mongol invasions. In Europe, the papacy is asserting unprecedented authority, and the mendicant orders are expanding their influence.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Southeast Asia (sparse documentation in Western sources)
Americas (no written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Death of Genghis Khan
events.description: Genghis Khan (Temüjin), founder of the Mongol Empire, dies in 1227 (exact date uncertain, possibly August). His death marks the end of an era of Mongol expansion under a single leader and the beginning of the succession crisis that will result in the division of the Mongol Empire among his sons. Ögedei Khan, his third son, will be chosen as his successor and will continue the Mongol conquests. Genghis Khan's death is a pivotal moment in world history, as it marks the transition from the unified Mongol Empire to the fragmented Khanates.

sources.name: Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles) by Rashid al-Din
Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Genghis Khan's death in 1227 is well-documented in multiple sources, though the exact date (possibly August) is uncertain.

events.title: Mongol campaigns against the Tangut kingdom of Xi Xia
events.description: Genghis Khan's forces conduct a major campaign against the Tangut kingdom of Xi Xia, which had resisted Mongol expansion. The campaign is one of the last major military operations conducted by Genghis Khan before his death. The Tangut kingdom will eventually fall to Mongol forces, and its territory will be incorporated into the Mongol Empire.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The campaign against Xi Xia is documented in later sources, though the exact dates and sequence of events are uncertain.

events.title: Reconquista: Siege of Úbeda
events.description: Christian forces from Castile, under Ferdinand III, besiege and capture the Islamic fortress of Úbeda in southern Iberia. The capture of Úbeda represents a significant territorial gain in the Reconquista and demonstrates the growing military strength of Christian kingdoms. The fortress becomes an important Christian stronghold in the region.

sources.name: Crónica de los Reyes de Castila (Chronicle of the Kings of Castile)

events.certainty_note: The capture of Úbeda in 1227 is documented in later chronicles and is generally accepted by historians.

events.title: Papal excommunication of Frederick II
events.region: Papal States and Holy Roman Empire
events.description: Pope Gregory IX excommunicates Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor, due to Frederick's failure to fulfill his crusade vow and his conflicts with papal authority. The excommunication marks the beginning of a long conflict between the papacy and Frederick II that will last for decades. This conflict represents a significant challenge to papal authority and demonstrates the growing power of secular rulers.
events.certainty_note: The excommunication of Frederick II in 1227 is well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Genghis Khan's death is uncertain; some sources place it in 1226 or 1228, though 1227 is the most widely accepted date. The significance of the campaign against Xi Xia is sometimes overstated, as the kingdom did not fall until 1227–1228 (or possibly later). The papal excommunication of Frederick II is sometimes dated to 1228 rather than 1227, depending on the source.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1227 is moderate, with European sources (particularly papal records and crusade chronicles) being relatively rich. East Asian sources depend on later compilations, particularly the Yuan Shi and Jami' al-tawarikh. Islamic sources provide some contemporary accounts of the Reconquista and Ayyubid affairs. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Europe and East Asia, with minimal coverage of other regions. The death of Genghis Khan is one of the most significant events of this year and is well-documented in multiple sources, though the exact date remains uncertain. Biases in surviving sources favor political and military events over economic, social, or cultural developments.

graph_edges.note: Death of Genghis Khan led to succession of Ögedei Khan and continuation of Mongol campaigns
Campaign against Xi Xia was one of Genghis Khan's last major military operations
Papal excommunication of Frederick II in 1227 set the stage for his Sixth Crusade in 1228
